message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 control measure stringency including testing has been among the highest globally in China. Psychosocial impact on pandemic workers in Shanghai, and their pandemic-related attitudes were investigated.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants in this cross-sectional study were healthcare providers (HCP) and other support workers. A Mandarin self-report survey was administered via Wenjuanxing between April-June 2022 during the omicron-wave lockdown. The Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) and Maslach Burnout Inventory were administered, as well as pandemic-specific questions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    887 workers participated, of which 691 (77.9%) were HCPs. They were working a mean of 6.25±1.24 days/week for 9.77±4.28 hours/day. Most participants were burnt-out, with 143(16.1%) moderately and 98(11.0%) seriously. Total PSS was 26.85±9.92/56, with 353(39.8%) participants having elevated stress. Workers perceived their families primarily as fully supportive (n=610, 68.8%), or also extremely concerned (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =203, 22.9%). Most wanted counselling and stress relief, but half(
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =430) reported no time for it; indeed, 2/3rds wanted a few days off to rest (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =601).Many workers perceived benefits: that they fostered more cohesive relationships (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =581, 65.5%), they will be more resilient (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =693, 78.1%), and were honored to serve (
                    <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                    =747, 84.2%).Negative impacts were greater in HCPs, those with economic insecurity, and that did not perceive benefit (all
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <.05).In adjusted analyses, those perceiving benefits showed significantly less burnout (OR=0.573, 95% CI=0.411 - 0.799), among other correlates.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Pandemic work, including among non-HCP, is stressful, but some can derive benefits.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: On the frontlines in Shanghai: Stress, burnout and perceived benefit among COVID-19 testers and other personnel during the Omicron wave lockdown
